title: Introduction to Programming – Core Data Structures Quiz

explanation.`[This, list, has, size] 4`: Correct. The four strings are added in order: "This", "list", "has", "size". A List preserves insertion order, so `toString()` produces `[This, list, has, size]`. After four `add()` calls, `size()` returns 4.
explanation.`[This, list, size] 3`: Incorrect. "has" is explicitly added between "list" and "size" — it is not missing. A List retains all added elements in insertion order, and `size()` would be 4, not 3.
explanation.`[This, has, size, list] 4`: Incorrect. "list" is added immediately after "This" and before "has". A List preserves the order in which elements were added, so this permutation cannot occur.
explanation.`[list, This, has, size] 4`: Incorrect. "This" is the first element added, so it appears first. A List preserves insertion order, meaning "list" cannot appear before "This".

questions.source_reference: W04 Core Data Structures – java.util.List<E> (ArrayList, add(), size(), toString())

explanation.`42; 24; 23; 53; `: Correct. A Stack follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle — the element pushed last is popped first. The push order is 53 → 23 → 24 → 42, so the pop order is the exact reverse: 42 → 24 → 23 → 53.
explanation.`53; 23; 24; 42; `: Incorrect. This is the push order, not the pop order. A Stack operates on LIFO, meaning elements are removed in the reverse of the order they were added.
explanation.`53; 42; 24; 23; `: Incorrect. A Stack does not sort its contents in descending order. It simply reverses the insertion order, so 42 (pushed last) is popped first and 53 (pushed first) is popped last.
explanation.`23; 24; 42; 53; `: Incorrect. A Stack does not sort its contents in ascending order. The correct pop order is 42, 24, 23, 53 — the reverse of the push sequence.

questions.source_reference: W04 Core Data Structures – Stack (LIFO principle, push(), pop(), isEmpty())

options: The Set contains the following elements: `Snow White`, `Cinderella`, `Ariel`, `Cinderella` and `Aurora`
The Set contains the following elements: `Aurora`, `Ariel`, `Snow White` and `Cinderella`
The Set contains the following elements: `Aurora`, `Ariel`, `Cinderella` and `Snow White`
correct_answer: The Set contains the following elements: `Aurora`, `Ariel`, `Snow White` and `Cinderella`
The Set contains the following elements: `Aurora`, `Ariel`, `Cinderella` and `Snow White`

explanation.The Set contains the following elements:: Incorrect. A Set stores only unique elements. The second attempt to add "Cinderella" is silently discarded, so the Set contains exactly four distinct elements, not five.
explanation.A `DuplicateEntryException` is thrown: Incorrect. The `Set` interface is designed to store unique entries. When `add()` is called with a value already present in the Set, the method simply returns `false` — no exception is thrown.
explanation.The Set contains the following elements:: Correct. A Set rejects duplicate entries, so only one "Cinderella" is stored. A HashSet does not guarantee any particular iteration order, so this is one valid ordering of the four unique elements.
Correct. The four unique elements are the same; only the iteration order differs. A HashSet does not guarantee a consistent ordering, so both this and the preceding option are valid outputs.

questions.source_reference: W04 Core Data Structures – java.util.Set<E> (HashSet, uniqueness, unordered iteration)

learning_objectives: Understand that a List preserves insertion order and correctly report its size and string representation
Apply the LIFO principle of a Stack to predict the output order of push() and pop() operations
Distinguish Set behaviour from List behaviour: uniqueness enforcement, silent duplicate rejection, and unordered HashSet iteration
